displayTitle: 2025-10-22 - Health Committee - Petition of Kathleen Walsh: Urgently find obstetric specialists for maternity services at Whakatāne Hospital
topic: Petition of Kathleen Walsh: Urgently find obstetric specialists for maternity services at Whakatāne Hospital
description: 22 October 2025 | Health Committee Petition of Kathleen Walsh: Urgently find obstetric specialists for maternity services at Whakatāne Hospital Health NZ Kate Grimwade, Te Hauora a Toi Chief Medical Officer Cath Cronin, Executive Regional Director Te Manawa Taki - Midland Region Briefing on assisted dying Dr. Jo Scott-Jones (some audio missing due to technical error) Petition of Fluoride Free New Zealand: Stop water fluoridation Ministry of Health Dr Richard Jaine, Deputy Director, Public Health Riana Clark, Clinical Chief Advisor, Oral Health Mark Ayson, Principal Advisor, Public Health Agency Petition of Lisa Er: Halt the Gene Technology Bill and set up a Commission of Inquiry Lisa Er
descriptionSummary: Petition of Kathleen Walsh: Urgently find obstetric specialists for maternity services at Whakatāne Hospital. Health NZ. Kate Grimwade, Te Hauora a Toi Chief Medical Officer

chunks.text: All right, we've got, um, Kate Grimwood and Cath Cronin. Welcome. Very good to have you joining Health committee today. We've got you in here for 10 minutes to talk about, um, the situation at Whakatane Hospital. I will hand it over to you, um, but also if you are able to leave us a couple of minutes for questions at the end, that'd be greatly appreciated too.

Thank you. Thank you. I'll Cap Cronin here. I'm the region regional executive director for teta. So we've started this, this work and by the end of December, January last year, we were in a position where we had, um, lost five SMOs to the obstetric and gynecology service in Whakatane.

So it means we had to, um, change our care to a primary birthing service and set up a system where women transferred to Ong Hospital for obstetric care. So we've been working over the last eight to nine months to restore secondary services to Whakatane. It's been a prime focus for us and definitely on our agenda to resume those services.

So we are in a position now, and I, I'll hand over to Kate to talk a little bit through the recruitment and how that's been going. But not only looking at a gynecology with the SMOs, but also strengthening our midwifery service. Working very closely with the community to make sure we've got that integrated start to stop of that whole pathway For women.

Our focus has been a lot on obstetrics. 'cause obviously a lot of the clinical risk was around those women and the babies, but we are also restoring our secondary gynecology services, which is a very important part, especially for the woman in Papa and right down the East Coast phase. So that's a little bit of an overview, but I'll hand over to Kate.

'cause I think the questions that we received were predominantly around our SMO recruitment, um, where I'm the Chief Medical Officer for HAR at to Bay of Plenty. So, um, I'll talk, um, a bit about the SMO side and then, um, a little bit about the other learnings perhaps. Um, so we have been successful in recruiting four SMOs and we've got a fifth who we're just waiting to see if she's gonna sign a contract.

And we're very hopeful that that will happen this week. One of those is already on board. He's been in, um, Targa for some months, as required by the medical council to start his practice, but is now successfully moving over to Whakatane. He does two days a week now at Whakatane and when that will step up with time. Um, and alongside that, the Todd Onga SMOs have been coming over and doing work in Whakatane.

So we've already been able to reestablish some day surgery for a lot of outpatient sessions. Antenatal care over in Whakatane, although we can't establish full services again, our second, um, SMO, uh, should be arriving within the next couple of weeks. He's just waiting for his immigration clearance to come into the country.

He's a very experienced, um, south African obstetrician and gynecologist. Um, and I think we'll have, um, a very good leadership qualities we'll bring to the service. 'cause We wanna reestablish a, a leader on site at Whakatane to make sure they have a very clear voice for ONG and Whakatane. Um, he again, will do a little bit of time at Targa, but because we've already got one person in place, we can transition him over to Whakatane a little bit sooner.

Um, so he'll probably spend the first four weeks or so just to settle into the New Zealand system and understand what that's about before we move him over. And the Targa visits will continue. So we, we are doing, um, about 50 sessions a month now in Whakatane and managing to get through a lot of work there. The next two SMOs both come at the beginning of January, so they're both from the states, um, and they are already, um, basically well set up to come now.

So we are pretty confident about their arrival. You can never be absolutely sure when you've got quite a lot of, um, balls in the ring, as it were. So, um, but they've been over, they've had lots of conversation with local community and then that's been a really big learning from bringing people in with the, uh, plan for them to stay long term has been about settling them in the community and, and how we, how we bring them in.

So when they come, they'll only need a very short period of con, may even just just be as assured as a week, um, because there will be much more wraparound staff at Whakatane by then. And then the, um, the fifth we hope to bring in, um, sort of, uh, February, march time if everything works out in that respect. So we'll go back to a full compliment.

Um, the, um, relationships with PGA are much more established though now. We have a new, um, leader for that service, medical leader for that service who's been incredible in this space, both recruiting, but establishing, also establishing those relationships. We have a new, um, chief midwife for the whole bay and a new midwifery leader for Whakatane itself.

So we feel that we are going in with really, um, a much more robust group of staff. We've done a large amount of work, um, talking with those on the ground in Whakatane, but also people who've been in Whakatane and have moved on. Um, and as well as large amounts of community engagement, um, holding forums across the Eastern Bay to understand and what the, um, priorities of the community are.

So we're taking those learnings away. We're also working with Laura, Ian and we rural generalism, um, space to look at rural generalist obstetricians and the model they use in the West coast to support our obstetric service once it's up and running and ensure sustainability. Happy to take any further questions, whichever way you wanna.

Thank you. I go over to Cam and I, you talked a lot about community engagement. I hear locally there has been a significant increase in that, especially since you came around, uh, Kath. So thank you for that. And so there, we'll just keep them in close contact now as we head towards March, April. Thank you. I'll come to, to cam. Yeah. Thank you j uh, my questions about a couple of things you raised there. So, to Manawa, Taki area, um, sharing services has, has this impacted on, um, how's this impacted on the wider region, like, uh, uh, as well?

And then on top of that, the, the question about the medical council requirement for, um, obstetrics to be in toga first. Could you explain why that is? Yeah, no problem. So not a great deal. We are more generally looking at regionalization of services and how we do share load across the region, though outside of Gyne, 'cause there are lots of other pinch points, obviously, that we'd like to work together. And, um, from the point of view of the medical council question, um, when someone first arrives, they need to have quite close working relationship with someone who's a fellow in that specialty that they're coming into with no people on the ground at all.

In Whakatane, that was impossible to start off with. So the initial person had to spend a fair bit of time working closely, and then that individual's gone with them to whakatane that supervisor to make, to ensure that practice is, is as is needed, but with the arrival of subsequent ones, and they're being more people on the ground in Whakatane, that time can be minimized and can be done much shorter.

Medical council's worked really closely with us and, and we've had a very good relationship with them so that we've minimized as much as possible. But even aside from the medical council requirements, it would be really quite with high risk to have an individual working on their own in whakatane with no other support there. So it, it really did make a lot of sense from the clinical risk point of view, as well as, um, the safety for someone who's come from a very different house setting to our own.

Thank you. Thank you very much. Um, we've gone over time, but, um, uh, well on Kath, it's been a, a notable improvement is what I've heard on the ground. Um, we have, um, online, uh, it's Dr. Rianna Clark. Um, I, uh, first of all, like to, um, discuss a little bit about the burden of rural health in New Zealand.

Uh, New Zealand has high rates of preventable tooth decay, um, particularly for our Maori and Pacific children and those living in deprived communities. Um, in the last year, there were over 10,000 children hospitalized for dental, um, treatment of which we, um, estimate 90% or over 90% were considered preventable. Of these preventable dental events, 62% were for children aged five to nine years.

41% were Maori and 14% of Pacific children, along with the health burden is also an economic burden of, um, for oral health in New Zealand. And action station, uh, study last year, um, showed that the economic burden of poor on health is estimated to be around 6.2 billion a year for New Zealand.

This includes, uh, over a hundred million dollars per year in health system costs, uh, 2.5 billion in lost productivity and $3.1 billion in reduced quality of life. A 2015 PRA report also estimated that if community water fluoridation was extended to water supplies that were not fluoridated, this would have the potential to result in an estimated net savings of more than $600 million under 20 years.

Much Immunity water fluoridation. So what is that? Um, it's the process of adjusting the natural occurring level of fluoride in the water supply to between 0.7 and one milligram per liter. Just for context, uh, New Zealand, um, fluoride occurs naturally in New Zealand water, but only at about 0.2, um, milligrams per liter, which is too low to be beneficial to oral health.

Currently, almost 60% of the New Zealand population has access to fluoride related drinking water and the water regulator to monitors fluoride water in the water supplies to ensure it does not supply the maximum acceptable value of 1.5 milligrams per liter. Community water fluoridation is the, uh, the role, the role of fluoride and mold has been examined around the world, including the New Zealand over the last 60 years.

Studies show that children and adults living in areas with community water fluoridation have significantly less tooth decay and people living in areas without community water fluoridation is endorsed by the World Health Organization and other international authorities as one of the most effective public health measures for the prevention of dental decay.

The 2015 review of the benefits and costs of community water fluoridation in New Zealand estimated that there would've been benefits. There's benefits to water audation including a 40% lower lifetime incidence of tooth decay among children and adolescents. 48% reduction in hospital admissions for tooth decay treatment among children aged zero to four years, a 21% reduction in tooth decay amongst adults, aged 18 to 44, and a 30% reduction in tooth decay amongst adults aged over 45

Oral health interventions. It's, it's important to have a suite of measures that they're the most effective and the most ProE equity in New Zealand. We have a, a community water fluoridation as part of a suite of measures that supports oral health, including free dental services for under eighteens, providing free toothbrushes and toothpaste for families with free schoolers and healthy eating support.

Going back to the, uh, free toothbrushes and toothpaste, uh, we that this was started in 2021 and while, um, at that time, uh, 40%, about 40% of zero to four year olds had improved, had, um, regular toothbrushing and studies show that, um, recently it's up to 60%, which is excellent because that shows that that intervention is working to some degree.

But it also shows that there's a large portion of people that are zero to four year olds that are not achieving this. Hence, the support of a suite of measures, including community water fluoridation, Community water fluoridation is safe. There have been four, um, New Zealand reviews of ev of the evidence since 2014.

Two from the Prime Minister's chief science advisors in 20, uh, 14 and 2021, which showed water fluoridation at the levels used in New Zealand posed no significant health risk in 2024. Just last year, the ministry provi, um, produce another evidence review that supported that conclusion showing the ongoing benefits of community water flu relation and that it provides equity.

The Ministry of Health then completed an additional review with some new evidence that had come to light recently, and that showed no change to the conclusions that were born above. The ministry continues to review new evidence and research as it comes to life.

So our final conclusion is really fairly simple. The safety and effectiveness of community water fluoridation as a public health measure has been consistently demonstrated across multiple studies of different designs by many years. Kilda questions. Great. Thank you. Members have questions? Jenny?

Okay. You can hear me? Yes, we've got you. Oh, that's great. Okay. Well, thank you for reading my written submission. In this short time, I'll, I'll just speak on the need for a commission of inquiry. I strongly support the establishment of a commission of inquiry into the gene technology bill. My position is grounded in the precautionary principle and a clear objection to allowing biotechnology corporations to risk contaminating New Zealand's agriculture and environment.

The bill involves complex, ethical, environmental, cultural, and economic issues that warrant independent evidence-based scrutiny. The 2001 Royal Commission recommended a proceed with caution approach, guidance that remains highly relevant today. Gene technologies include advance advances like CRISPR that have wide reaching implications in many areas of our lives across agriculture, ecosystems, animals, plants, microbes, food production, agriculture, ag, agrichemicals, and medicine.

A commission of inquiry would ensure democratic oversight prevent undue influence from industry or narrow political interests, and provide clear recommendations on law and policy, particularly around the has no act. This process is too important to be rushed.

A modern transparent inquiry would allow New Zealand to balance scientific innovation with public values, environmental protection, and long-term sustainability. Given the seriousness and scope of this issue, an independent commission is essential to ensure robust precautionary safeguards that protect farmers, consumers, farmers, the environment and future generations.

Over to Claire. Um, to, um, unregulated use of GM would involve taking unacceptable risks with human and environmental health, and with our cultural heritage, it would also compromise consumer choice and our export options. This was written in 2001 by the Royal Commission, uh, on genetic modification.

Yet the current gene technology bill has ignored that warning by allowing unrestricted use of GM technologies through its exemptions.
